title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
How to save tax on an additional 50,000 income under section 80CCD (1B)?
Have you already exhausted your tax-saving options under Section 80C and are looking for ways to save tax on an extra ₹50,000 of income to lower your tax burden? Simply invest in the National Pension System (NPS) and avail of an additional tax deduction of ₹50,000. Section 80CCD(1B) allows a tax deduction of ₹50,000 precisely for NPS contributions along with the ₹1.5 lakh limit available in Section 80C1. With full utilization of the deductions of Section 80C, along with an additional NPS investment, an individual can claim a total deduction of about Rs 2 lakh in a specific financial year. By investing in NPS, you can easily reduce your taxable income and get long-term retirement benefits, making it a smart option to save both for the future and on taxes1.
All about Section 80CCD(1B):
Section 80CCD(1B) of the Income Tax Act of 1961 provides an additional tax deduction of up to ₹50,000 for contributing to the National Pension System (NPS). You can avail of the deduction of ₹50,000 u/s 80CCD (1B) is beyond the ₹1.5 lakh income tax deduction of Section 80C/80CCC/80CCD (1). Hence, an individual can claim a maximum tax deduction going up to is ₹2 lakhs u/s 80CCD(1B) and Section 80C/80CCC/80CCD (1)2.
However, the deductions is available only under the old tax regime. To avail of the exemption u/s 115BAC(1A)2, an individual has to shift out of the new tax regime.
National Pension Scheme (NPS)
The National Pension System (NPS) is a government-backed pension scheme open to both salaried as well as self-employed individuals. It is a savings scheme designed for retirement which offers dual benefits of tax savings when you are working and steady income flow after retirement1.
Contributions made to NPS are invested in a variety of securities, including the equity market. NPS and equity exposure are considered one of the most affordable investment options. As the returns on NPS investment depend on the performance of the market, there are no guaranteed returns. However, NPS is known to have consistently delivered some of the highest returns over time1.
Types of NPS Accounts
The National Pension System (NPS) offers two types of accounts for individual investors:
Tier I Account (Mandatory/Pension Account)
The Tier I account is the primary NPS account for long-term retirement savings. Contributions to this NPS account qualify for tax benefits through Sections 80C and 80CCD(1B) of the Income Tax Act of 1961. The contributions to this account have a fixed tenure of a lock-in period until the NPS subscriber actually reaches the age of 60 years. However, partial withdrawals are allowed, subject to the given terms and conditions. You can invest up to ₹2 lakhs in a Tier I account and get a deduction on the entire amount u/s 80C and 80CCD(1B)1.
The contributions to this account can be made regularly or in a lump sum. There is no limit on the total contributions that can be made, but the minimum contribution required to keep the NPS account active is Rs. 500. Upon retirement or reaching the age of 60, a part of the corpus can be withdrawn as a lump sum (up to 60%), and the remaining amount must be used to purchase an annuity to provide a regular income stream3.
Tier II Account (Voluntary/ Optional Account)
The Tier II account is considered a voluntary savings account that functions just like a savings account with the NPS. It allows for flexibility, as there is no specific lock-in period like the Tier I account. However, no tax deductions are available for contributions to the Tier II account u/s 80C or 80CCD(1B)3. Only Central government employees can claim a tax deduction on contributions made to a Tier 2 account 1.
Eligibility of individuals under Section 80CCD(1B)
Here are the eligibility requirements for claiming a deduction u/s 80CCD(1B):
Available to both salaried and self-employed individual taxpayers
It is applicable to individuals who contribute to the National Pension Scheme (NPS).
Available only to individuals filing their income tax returns using the old regime of taxation
Only the contributions to the Tier I NPS account qualify for deduction u/s 80CCD(1B). Contributions to the Tier II account do not qualify1.
Age limits apply to opening an NPS account. You can open an account if you fall into one of these categories:
Resident individuals that are between the ages of 18 and 70 years
NRIs, or Non-Resident Indians, that are between the ages of 18-70 years. However, if the citizenship of the NRI changes after starting an NPS account, the scheme will be terminated1.
Starting from the financial year 2025-26, NPS Vatsalya account contributions will receive the same tax benefits as NPS u/s 80CCD(1B)1.
How to make an NPS investment to get the extra Rs 50,000 deduction4
To get the extra ₹50,000 amount of deduction with Section 80CCD(1B) for your National Pension Scheme (NPS) investment, you need to contribute to your NPS Tier I account. The investment to the NPS account should be done in your individual name2. Here is the step-by-step guide on making an NPS investment to enjoy the extra Rs 50,000 deduction:
Step 1: Open an NPS Account (If Not Already Open)
To avail of the Rs. 50,000 deductions through Section 80CCD(1B), you must have an NPS Tier I account. You can open an NPS account through any of the following methods:
Online (eNPS)
The easiest way to open an NPS account is online through the eNPS portal provided by NSDL (National Securities Depository Limited). Simply go to the official eNPS website: https://enps.nsdl.com/eNPS/ and use Aadhaar-based eKYC to open an account quickly. After successful registration, you will get a PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number), which can be used for making investments and tracking your NPS account.
Through Point of Presence (PoP)
You can open an NPS account by registeringin PERDA online or through physical  mode. You will need to submit the necessary KYC documents for this process.
Step 2: Contribute to Your NPS Tier I Account
To avail of the additional Rs. 50,000 deductions available in Section 80CCD(1B), you need to make a contribution to your Tier I NPS account. Central Government employees can also contribute to Tier II accounts to claim deductions.
You can make multiple contributions throughout the year. The minimum contribution required to keep the NPS account active is Rs. 500. However, to maximize your deduction, you should contribute at least Rs. 50,000 to your NPS Tier I account in a financial year.
Step 3: Modes of Making the Contribution
You can contribute to your NPS Tier I account in any of the following ways:
Online Transfer (eNPS):
Log in to your eNPS account on the official website.
You can make direct payments from your bank account to your NPS Tier I account via online payment options or use debit cards, net banking, or other modes supported by the platform to make the payment.
Physical Mode through Point of Presence (POP)
If you opened the account through a POP, you can visit their online portal/website branch to make a contribution. You can pay through a demand draft, cheque, or make an online transfer.
Employer’s Contributions
If your employer contributes to your NPS account, it will be treated separately u/s 80CCD(2). The ₹50,000 additional deduction u/s 80CCD(1B) applies only to your personal contributions.
Step 4: Claim the Deduction in Your ITR
Ensure to file Income Tax Returns under the old regime. When you file your Income Tax Return (ITR), mention the contribution made to your NPS account in the relevant sections. For Section 80CCD(1B), you will find a section for deductions under Chapter VI-A in the ITR. Enter the amount you contributed to your NPS Tier I account (up to Rs. 50,000), under the specific field for NPS (Section 80CCD(1B)),
Step 5:  Documents for Tax Filing
While filing your tax return, keep the following documents as proof for your NPS contribution:
PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number).
Contribution receipts or bank statements showing the NPS payment.
Acknowledgement from NPS (if applicable).
Along with your PAN Card, Aadhaar Card and bank account details1.
Conclusion
NPS is a popular choice for individuals looking to build a retirement fund and secure regular monthly income. NPS is a win-win scheme where you get immediate tax savings plus a long-term retirement benefit, making it a smart and simple strategy to maximize your tax benefits while securing your financial future.
Sources:
https://cleartax.in/s/section-80-ccd-1b
https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/wealth/tax/how-nps-investment-of-rs-50000-can-help-you-save-more-income-tax/articleshow/108421064.cms?from=mdr
https://npstrust.org.in/benefits-of-nps
https://npstrust.org.in/sites/default/files/inline-images/05-All-Citz-Mdl-Faq.pdf
Disclaimer
The views expressed in this article is not to be construed as professional advice and users are advised to seek independent professional/expert advice before making any decisions based on the same. Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Ltd., Regd. office Address: Bajaj Allianz House, Airport Road, Yerawada, Pune - 411006, Reg. No.: 116, CIN: U66010PN2001PLC015959, Call us on toll free No.: 1800 209 7272, Mail us: customercare@bajajallianz.co.in
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility
full_pdf_text:
Saving tax on an additional 50,000 income under section 80CCD (1B)?
Have you already exhausted your tax-saving options under Section 80C and are looking for ways to save tax on an extra ₹50,000 of income to lower your tax liability ? You can choose the National Pension System (NPS) and avail an additional tax deduction of ₹50,000. Section 80CCD(1B) allows a tax deduction of ₹50,000 for NPS contributions along with the ₹1.5 lakh limit available in Section 80CCD (1)1. With full utilization of the deductions of Section 80CCD(1), along with an additional NPS investment, an individual can claim a total deduction of about Rs 2 lakh in a specific financial year. NPS offers added tax benefits that help taxpayers lower their tax liability.
All about Section 80CCD(1B):
Section 80CCD(1B) of the Income Tax Act of 1961 provides an additional tax deduction of up to ₹50,000 for contributing to the National Pension System (NPS). You can avail of the deduction of ₹50,000 u/s 80CCD (1B) which is beyond the ₹1.5 lakh income tax deduction of Section 80C/80CCC/80CCD (1). Hence, an individual can claim a maximum tax deduction of upto ₹2 lakhs by combining sections 80CCD(1B) and Section 80C/80CCC/80CCD (1)2.
However, the deductions are available only under the old tax regime.
National Pension Scheme (NPS)
The National Pension System (NPS) is a government-backed pension scheme open to both salaried as well as self-employed individuals. It is a savings scheme designed for retirement which offers dual benefits of tax savings when you are working and steady income flow after retirement1.
Contributions made to NPS are invested in a variety of securities, including the equity market. As the returns on NPS investment depend on the performance of the market, there are no guaranteed returns
Types of NPS Accounts
The National Pension System (NPS) offers two types of accounts :
1. Tier I Account (Mandatory/Pension Account)
The Tier I account is the primary NPS account for long-term retirement savings. Contributions to this NPS account qualify for tax benefits through Sections 80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B) of the Income Tax Act of 1961. The contributions to this account have a fixed tenure of a lock-in period until the NPS subscriber reaches the age of 60 years. However, partial withdrawals are allowed, subject to the given terms and conditions. On an investment of ₹2 lakhs in a Tier I account a deduction on the entire amount u/s 80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B) can be obtained 1.
The contributions to this account can be made regularly or in a lump sum. There is no limit on the total contributions that can be made, but the minimum contribution required to keep the NPS account active is Rs. 5005. Upon retirement or reaching the age of 60 or on superannuation , a part of the corpus can be withdrawn as a lump sum (up to 60%), and the remaining amount must be used to purchase an annuity to provide a regular income stream6. However, if the amount is less than ₹5 lakhs, the full amount can be withdrawn in a lump sum.6
2. Tier II Account (Voluntary/ Optional Account)
The Tier II account is considered a voluntary savings account that functions just like a savings account with the NPS. It allows for flexibility, as there is no specific lock-in period like the Tier I account7. However, no tax deductions are available for contributions to the Tier II account 3. Only Central government employees can claim a tax deduction on contributions made to a Tier 2 account 1.
Eligibility of individuals under Section 80CCD(1B)
Individual Taxpayers who file Income Tax Returns under the Old Tax Regime are eligible to claim deductions under Section 80CCD(1B) :
Age limits apply to opening an NPS account. You can open an account if you fall into one of these categories:
● Resident individuals that are between the ages of 18 and 70 years ● NRIs, or Non-Resident Indians, that are between the ages of 18-70 years. However, if the citizenship of the NRI changes after starting an NPS account, the scheme will be terminated1.
Starting from the financial year 2025-26*, NPS Vatsalya account contributions will receive the same tax benefits as NPS u/s 80CCD(1B)1.
Other points to keep in mind to claim under section 80CCD(1B):
● Available to both salaried and self-employed individual taxpayers ● It is applicable to individuals who contribute to the National Pension Scheme (NPS). ● Contributions to the Tier I NPS account qualify for deduction u/s 80CCD(1B) and 80 CCD (1) . Contributions to NPS tier II accounts are eligible for tax deductions if they are made by a Central Government employee 1.
NPS investment to get the extra Rs 50,000 deduction4
The contribution towards NPS Tier I account qualify for an extra ₹50,000 amount of deduction under Section 80CCD(1B) . . The investment to the NPS account should be done in your individual name2. Here is the step-by-step guide on making an NPS investment to enjoy the extra Rs 50,000 deduction:
Step 1: Open an NPS Account (If Not Already Open)
To avail of the Rs. 50,000 deductions through Section 80CCD(1B), you must have an NPS Tier I account. You can open an NPS account through any of the following methods:
● Online (eNPS) You can open an NPS account online in a few steps. You can fill out the account opening form, submit the documents and make a contribution to open the account.
● Through Point of Presence (PoP) You can open an NPS account by registering in PFRDA online or through physical mode. You will need to submit the necessary KYC documents for this process.
Step 2: Contribute to Your NPS Tier I Account
The contribution towards NPS Tier I account qualify for an extra ₹50,000 amount of deduction under Section 80CCD(1B).
You can make multiple contributions throughout the year. The minimum contribution required for NPS Tier I Account is Rs. 500.
Step 3: Modes of Making the Contribution
You can contribute to your NPS Tier I account in any of the following ways:
1. Online Transfer (eNPS):
o Log in to your eNPS account on the official website. o You can make direct payments from your bank account to your NPS Tier I account via online payment options or use debit cards, net banking, or other modes supported by the platform to make the payment. 2. Physical Mode through Point of Presence (POP)
If you opened the account through a POP, then you can visit the registered servcice provider and deposit a cheque or bank slip with the NPS Contribution slip. 3. Employer’s Contributions
If your employer contributes to your NPS account, it will be treated separately u/s 80CCD(2). The ₹50,000 additional deduction u/s 80CCD(1B) applies only to your personal contributions.
Step 4: Claim the Deduction in Your ITR
Ensure to file Income Tax Returns under the old regime. When you file your Income Tax Return (ITR), mention the contribution made to your NPS account in the relevant sections. For Section 80CCD(1B), you will find a section for deductions under Chapter VI-A in the ITR. Enter the amount you contributed to your NPS Tier I account (up to Rs. 50,000), under the specific field for NPS (Section 80CCD(1B)),8
Step 5: Documents for Tax Filing
While filing your tax return, keep the following documents as proof for your NPS contribution:
● PAN Card, ● Aadhaar Card ● Bank account details1.
Conclusion
NPS is a popular choice for individuals looking to build a retirement fund and secure regular monthly income. With NPS you can save tax and also plan for retirement. So, assess your financial needs and make an informed choice.
Sources:
1. https://cleartax.in/s/section-80-ccd-1b 2. https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/wealth/tax/how-nps-investment-of-rs-50000-can-help-
you-save-more-income-tax/articleshow/108421064.cms?from=mdr 3. https://npstrust.org.in/benefits-of-nps 4. https://npstrust.org.in/sites/default/files/inline-images/05-All-Citz-Mdl-Faq.pdf 5. https://npscra.nsdl.co.in/all-faq-contribution.php 6. https://npscra.nsdl.co.in/nps-exit/ 7. https://npscra.nsdl.co.in/features-and-benefits-of-nps.php/all-citizens-faq.php 8. https://www.indiafilings.com/learn/nps-tax-exemption/
Disclaimer
The views expressed in this article is not to be construed as professional advice and users are advised to seek independent professional/expert advice before making any decisions based on the same. Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Ltd., Regd. office Address: Bajaj Allianz House, Airport Road, Yerawada, Pune - 411006, Reg. No.: 116, CIN: U66010PN2001PLC015959, Call us on toll free No.: 1800 209 7272, Mail us: customercare@bajajallianz.co.in
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility
* The above content is subject to the passing of the Finance Bill 2025 in the parliament. BJAZ-WEB-ECNF-14037/25

changes.word_original: NPS is a win-win scheme where you get immediate tax savings plus a long-term retirement benefit, making it a smart and simple strategy to maximize your tax benefits while securing your financial future.
changes.pdf_final: With NPS you can save tax and also plan for retirement.
So, assess your financial needs and make an informed choice.
